data.name: Fluffy Chestnut and Nutmeg Pancakes
data.description: Light, airy pancakes crafted with earthy chestnut flour, rich sour cream, and a hint of warm nutmeg. Whipped egg whites ensure a delicate texture, perfect for any time of day.

refrigerator.notes: Store in an airtight container layered with parchment paper.

freezer.notes: Freeze flat before transferring to a sealed bag.

storage.reheating: Warm in a toaster or oven at 175 C (350 F) until heated through.

items.name: chestnut flour
items.notes: Store in freezer to prevent rancidity

instructions.text: Sift the chestnut flour, granulated sugar, baking powder, baking soda, ground nutmeg, and kosher salt through a fine mesh sieve into a large mixing bowl to remove any clumps.
In a medium mixing bowl, whisk together the sour cream, whole milk, egg yolks, and melted butter until completely smooth.
Pour the wet ingredients into the dry ingredients and stir gently just until combined. Let the batter rest for 5 minutes.
While the batter rests, whip the egg whites in a clean bowl using a hand mixer or whisk until soft peaks form.

doneness_cues.visual: Whites hold their shape but the tips fold over when the whisk is lifted

instructions.text: Gently fold the whipped egg whites into the rested batter in two batches using a spatula, being careful not to deflate the trapped air.
Preheat a non-stick skillet over medium heat, approximately 175°C (350°F). Melt a small portion of the solid butter to lightly coat the surface.
Pour roughly 60ml portions of batter onto the skillet. Cook for 2 to 3 minutes until bubbles appear on the surface and the edges look set.

doneness_cues.visual: Bubbles popping on the surface, edges slightly dry and opaque

instructions.text: Carefully flip the pancakes using a spatula and cook for an additional 1 to 2 minutes on the second side until golden brown and cooked through to an internal temperature of 93°C (200°F).

doneness_cues.tactile: Springs back slightly when pressed gently in the center

instructions.text: Transfer the pancakes to a warm plate. Serve immediately with extra butter, maple syrup, or a dollop of fresh ricotta.

troubleshooting.likely_cause: Egg whites were deflated during folding or batter was overmixed
troubleshooting.prevention: Fold whites gently in a figure-eight motion just until no large white streaks remain
troubleshooting.fix: Cannot be fixed once cooked, but add a tiny splash of milk to remaining batter if it seems too thick

troubleshooting.likely_cause: Chestnut flour has oxidized and gone rancid
troubleshooting.prevention: Store chestnut flour in the freezer and taste a small pinch before beginning the recipe

chef_notes: Chestnut flour has a short shelf life and goes rancid quickly due to its natural oils. Always store it tightly sealed in the freezer.
Sifting is non-negotiable here. Chestnut flour naturally clumps in the bag and those dense pockets will ruin the delicate texture we are aiming for.
The acidity in the sour cream activates the baking soda, which works in tandem with the whipped egg whites to provide the lift necessary to overcome the inherently dense nature of chestnut flour.
data.cultural_context: Originating from the Apennine mountains of Tuscany and Emilia-Romagna, traditional chestnut pancakes, known as Necci, are typically cooked in special cast iron plates over a fire. This modern iteration introduces sour cream and leavening agents to transform the traditionally dense flatbread into a fluffy breakfast or savory dinner centerpiece.
